to hypoxia via zinc transportation inhibition drives
angiogenesis

In brief
Li and colleagues show that inhibiting
zinc transportation activates AMPK in
stem cell-derived islets, enhancing
functional maturation, hypoxia tolerance,
and graft vascularization. This pre-
adaptation strategy overcomes ischemic
graft failure, a major barrier to diabetes
cell therapy, and offers a translatable
approach to improve transplantation
outcomes.
Highlights
Excessive zinc inhibits AMPK activation in SC-islets
Chemical-inhibiting zinc transportation activates AMPK
signaling in SC-islets
AMPK activation enhances maturation, hypoxia tolerance,
and VEGFA expression
Preconditioned SC-islets show improved vascularization,
graft survival, and function

SUMMARY
The clinical application of human stem cell-derived islet organoids (SC-islets) is hindered by immaturity and
ischemia-induced dysfunction post-transplantation. Hypoxia-driven angiogenesis is a common adaptation,
but the metabolic fragility of SC-islet β cells leads to early functional damage and suppressed vascular endo-
thelial growth factor A (VEGFA) expression, thereby delaying vascularization and causing graft loss. The key
challenge in SC-islet transplantation is how to prevent hypoxia-induced stress and promote rapid angiogen-
esis. We found that excessive zinc in SC-islet β cells induces oxidative modification that inhibits AMP-acti-
vated protein kinase (AMPK) activity. Chemical inhibition of zinc transportation activates AMPK, enhances
functional maturation, improves hypoxia resistance, and increases hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF1A)-inde-
pendent VEGFA expression to facilitate endothelial cell integration. In diabetic animal models, this approach
significantly improved hypoxia resistance, accelerated angiogenesis, and enhanced glycemic control. Our
findings demonstrate that chemical inhibition of zinc transportation boosts SC-islet functional competence,
offering a potential strategy to advance pre-adaptation to stress in regenerative medicine.
INTRODUCTION
Diabetes mellitus, a global health crisis affecting 589 million peo-
ple worldwide,1 is characterized by the loss or dysfunction of
pancreatic
\beta
cells, leading to impaired glucose homeostasis.
The generation and transplantation of human pluripotential
stem cell-derived islet organoids (SC-islets) represent a crucial
advancement in stem cell research, with the goal of establishing
a promising approach for diabetes treatment.2–9 However, the
clinical application of SC-islets is hindered by three major chal-
lenges, i.e., functional immaturity, susceptibility to hypoxia
stress, and lack of vascularization post-transplantation, ulti-
mately compromising therapeutic efficacy.
Angiogenesis is a critical determinant of islet transplantation
success. Transplanted grafts, including primary islets and SC-is-
lets, initially face severe hypoxia due to disrupted vascular
connections post-transplantation, resulting in more than
islet graft loss.10–19
However, hypoxia-inducible factor 1α
(HIF1A)—a master regulator of adaptive responses to oxygen
( \mathsf { O } _ { 2 } )
deprivation—stabilizes under low
tension and drives
vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGFA) expression in
many cell types, such as cardiomyocytes, neurons, and

hepatocytes, to promote angiogenesis.20–25 Yet, this universal
hypoxia-adaptation mechanism fails to protect pancreatic
\beta
cells. Unlike these hypoxia-resilient cells,
\beta
cells exhibit extreme
metabolic fragility; their reliance on mitochondrial respiration for
glucose-stimulated insulin secretion results in rapid
deple-
tion, rendering them uniquely susceptible to hypoxic damage,
accelerated apoptosis, and functional impairment under
compromised
supply.26–30 While the hypoxic environment
of transplantation sites can promote angiogenesis, early-stage
hypoxia exerts detrimental effects on SC-islets, including oxida-
tive stress, apoptosis, and impaired function.10,12,31,32 This par-
adoxical effect limits the expression of VEGFA, thereby creating
a barrier to effective graft integration. Therefore, it is critical to
develop a hypoxia-tolerant approach to induce VEGFA expres-
sion, enabling rapid angiogenesis at the beginning of SC-islet
transplantation. However, how to resolve this discrepancy re-
mains elusive.
AMPK, a central hub in cellular energy homeostasis, is critically
dysregulated in type 2 diabetes (T2D), where its diminished
activity exacerbates pancreatic islet dysfunction and metabolic
stress.33,34
Restoring AMPK signaling has emerged as a therapeu-
tic strategy, as its activation enhances glucose utilization, reverses
insulin resistance, and promotes
\beta
cell maturation while conferring
resilience against oxidative and endoplasmic reticulum
stress.33,35–37 Notably, AMPK orchestrates hypoxia adaptation
and vascular remodeling by upregulating VEGFA expression,
enhancing endothelial cell functionality and reprogramming meta-
bolic responses to
deprivation.38–40 Intriguingly, zinc is a trace
element essential for insulin crystallization in
\beta
cells.41,42 While
the basal level of zinc is indispensable for cell function and survival,
its excessive accumulation in SC-islets inhibits insulin secretion43
and, in neurons, disrupts AMPK activity.44,45 This zinc-AMPK inter-
play is further highlighted by studies demonstrating that zinc
chelation rescues neuronal survival by suppressing zinc-mediated
excitotoxicity and restoring AMPK phosphorylation, indicating a
translatable approach to mitigate zinc overload in SC-islets.46,47
Building on this paradigm, we propose that pharmacological inhi-
bition of zinc transportation in SC-islets may reactivate AMPK
signaling, thereby simultaneously addressing three critical barriers
to transplantation success; these include enhancing functional
maturation by alleviating zinc-induced secretory inhibition,
bolstering hypoxia resistance through AMPK-driven metabolic
adaptation, and accelerating graft vascularization.
Here, this study addresses three critical challenges, i.e.,
immaturity, ischemia-induced failure, and lack of vasculariza-
tion of SC-islets post-transplantation. Specifically, with single-
cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and single-nucleus assay
for transposase-accessible chromatin sequencing (snATAC-
seq), we show that zinc accumulation in SC-islet
\beta
cells
inhibits AMPK activity, and its chemical inhibition activates
AMPK, downregulates HIF1A, and upregulates HIF1A-
independent VEGFA expression—thereby promoting endothe-
lial cell integration and angiogenesis. Importantly, in the
diabetic animal model, this approach improves hypoxia resis-
tance, accelerates graft angiogenesis, and enhances glycemic
control.
RESULTS
Excessive zinc induces elevated ROS and consequent
oxidation of AMPK in SC-islets
Zinc, an essential trace element, plays a pivotal role in
insulin crystallization, storage, and secretion in pancreatic
\beta
cells.41,42,48 By contrast, excessive zinc disrupts AMPK activity
in neural cells.44,45
Based on these, we hypothesized that zinc
overload in SC-islets may induce AMPK inactivation and hamper
SC-islet function. To test this, we generated SC-islets using
three independent human embryonic stem cell (hESC) lines,
Mel1-reporter (Mel1-R, INSGFP/W-NKX6.1mCherry/mCherry, labeling
SC-islet
\beta
cells), HuES8, and ZB13AD-B (AD-B), through a
seven-stage differentiation protocol. This process sequentially
yielded definitive endoderm, pancreatic progenitor, endocrine
progenitor, and SC-islets with a cellular composition compara-
ble to human primary islets (Figures 1A–1E). During differentia-
tion, zinc accumulation progressively intensified (Figures 1F,
1G, and S1A), peaking in SC-islets. Strikingly, these SC-islets
exhibited pronounced
\beta
cell heterogeneity characterized by
spatially distinct zinc- and insulin-enriched subpopulations
(Figures 1H and 1I), revealing an inverse correlation between
excessive zinc and insulin expression, suggestive of zinc-medi-
ated insulin expression suppression. In addition, we found that
zinc levels in

Current studies report that zinc induces reactive oxygen
species (ROS) accumulation,50 which drives AMPK oxidation
and aggregation.51 To validate this mechanism in SC-islets, we
applied zinc ionophore-zinc pyrithione (ZnPTO, a membrane-
permeable zinc coordination complex52,53) to mimic pathologi-
cally elevated zinc transportation. ZnPTO treatment triggered
zinc-dependent ROS elevation (Figures 1J and 1K), while zinc-
enriched regions showed diminished AMPKα expression54
(Figures 1L and 1M). Furthermore, ZnPTO treatment exacer-
bated AMPKα oxidation, providing mechanistic evidence for
zinc-mediated AMPK inactivation (Figures 1N and 1O). Collec-
tively, these findings suggest dysregulated zinc homeostasis
as a key driver of AMPK inactivation in SC-islets, positioning
zinc transportation inhibition as a viable strategy to enhance
AMPK activity and functional maturation.
Chemical inhibition of zinc transportation prevents
AMPK oxidation in SC-islets
To identify compounds capable of reducing zinc transportation
and consequently enhancing AMPK activity and functional matu-
ration in SC-islets, we performed compound screening and vali-
dation of zinc transportation inhibitors using both SC-islets and
human primary islets as experimental models. In the first
screening phase, we seeded INSGFP/W-NKX6.1mCherry/mCherry
Mel1-R-derived SC-islets into 96-well plates, followed by the
treatment of compounds from a small-molecule library, including
1,685 chemical inhibitors. Following 48-h incubation, zinc levels
were quantified via zinquin-based fluorescence imaging, with
decreased zinquin fluorescence intensity serving as the primary
readout for zinc transportation inhibition (Figures 2A and S1D).
From the screening, SU6656 (25 nM) emerged as a candidate,
effectively inhibiting zinc transportation in wild-type (WT) SC-is-
lets while increasing the proportion of

β cells in
HuES8- or AD-B-derived SC-islets, with appropriate controls
included (Figures 2B–2D, S1E, and S1F). After SU6656
treatment, which reduces zinc transportation, the number of
INS-GFP strong cells significantly increased, while INS-GFP
weak cells markedly decreased (Figures S1G and S1H),
revealing SC-islet
\beta
cell heterogeneity based on the diversity of
zinc accumulation, which is consistent with our previous study
with human islets.55 Its zinc transportation inhibitory activity
was first confirmed in human primary islets (Figure S1I). To
further rigorously validate the zinc transportation inhibitory effi-
cacy of SU6656, we employed the genetic (WFS1 knockout
[KO], amplifying zinc transportation55) and ZnPTO-treated SC-
islets as the elevated zinc transportation models. Importantly,
SU6656 effectively normalized zinc transportation in both patho-
logical models, establishing its robust inhibition of zinc transpor-
tation (Figures S1J–S1M).
Next, we examined whether the zinc transportation inhibitor
can activate AMPK in zinc-overloaded model; we found that
SU6656 reversed ZnPTO-treatment-induced ROS accumulation
(Figures 2E and S2A), prevented AMPK oxidation (Figures 2F and
S2B–S2D), and restored AMPK activation (Figures 2G and S2E).
Furthermore, given that zinc transportation in
\beta
cells is predom-
inantly mediated by the zinc transporter ZnT8,56 we employed
ZnT8 KO to abolish zinc transportation. Consistent with chemical
treatment of SU6656, compared with WT SC-islets,
SC-islets with abolished zinc transportation43 also showed
elevated AMPK activity, while
SC-islets with elevated
zinc transportation55,57
displayed reduced AMPK activation
(Figures S2F–S2J). Collectively, these data further confirmed
that zinc transportation inhibition promoted AMPK activity.
Importantly, SU6656 treatment effectively increased AMPK
activity not only in WT SC-islets (Figures 2H and S2H) but
also in zinc-overloaded model
SC-islets (Figure S2J).
However, in
SC-islets where zinc transportation
was abolished, SU6656 failed to amplify additional AMPK
activation (Figures 2H, S2F, and S2H). These results suggest
that SU6656’s AMPK-activating effect in SC-islets was
dependent on zinc transportation inhibition. Another notable
role of AMPK is the regulation of mitochondrial function,58 a crit-
ical determinant factor of SC-islet maturation.59,60

tion (Figures 2I–2L and S2K–S2V) and enhanced oxygen
consumption rates (OCRs) in SC-islets (Figures 2M, 2N, and
S2W–S2Y), demonstrating restored mitochondrial function.

SU6656 accelerates SC-islet functional maturation
through AMPK activation
Since
\beta
cell maturation is typically accompanied by metabolic
adaptation and mitochondrial functional optimization, we next
investigated whether SU6656 could promote SC-islet matura-
tion via AMPK activation.61 To test this, we performed scRNA-
seq and snATAC-seq analysis on DMSO- or SU6656-treated
SC-islets (Figure 3A). The scRNA-seq and snATAC-seq data
were integrated based on gene expression profiling and gene
activity scores. Using dimensionality reduction and unsuper-
vised clustering, we resolved distinct cellular clusters, which
were then annotated as six cell types based on using established
markers: α cells (GCG),
\beta
cells (INS), δ cells (SST), enterochro-
maffin cells (FEV), progenitor cells (SOX9), and proliferating cells
(TOP2A) (Figures 3B and S3A–S3C). The resulting SC-islet
cellular composition resembled human islet heterogeneity.62
Specifically, SU6656 treatment increased the
\beta
cell population
(Figures 3C, S3D, and S3E), directly extending our earlier obser-
vation of its ability to increase
W { \sf S } ^ { + } \sf N K X 6 . 1 ^ { + } \beta
cells (Figure 2D).
Notably, single-cell transcriptomic analysis revealed a marked
reduction in SLC30A8 expression following SU6656 treatment
(Figure S3F), indicating that the inhibitory effect of SU6656 on
zinc transportation is mediated through reducing transcriptional
suppression of SLC30A8. To further validate the inhibitory
mechanism of SU6656 on ZnT8, we utilized a Mel1
( I N S ^ { w / G F P } )
ZnT8::RFP-reporter line, enabling visualization of ZnT8 expres-
sion and localization as a fusion protein. Following SU6656
treatment, both ZnT8 mRNA levels and ZnT8::RFP fluorescence
intensity markedly decreased, suggesting that SU6656 sup-
presses ZnT8 expression (Figures S3G–S3J).
To further determine whether SU6656 can activate AMPK
independently of SLC30A8, we next examined its effect in
the absence of functional ZnT8. To test the specificity of
SU6656 on AMPK activation by inhibition of ZnT8-mediated
zinc transportation, we found that SU6656, acting as a chem-
ical inhibitor of zinc transportation, specifically blocked ZnT8-
mediated zinc transportation (Figures S3K and S3L). Genetic
abolishment of zinc transportation (by SLC30A8/ZnT8 KO)
leads to AMPK activation, suggesting an inhibitory role of
zinc transportation. Consistently, chemical inhibition of zinc
transportation (SU6656 treatment) also elevates AMPK activ-
ity, and additionally, SU6656 counteracts the inhibitory role
by excessive zinc on AMPK activation. Moreover, SU6656
no longer produced additional AMPK activation in the
background (Figure S3M), demonstrating that
SU6656 specifically targets ZnT8-mediated zinc transporta-
tion to activate AMPK.
We subsequently focused our analysis on
\beta
cells. Multi-omics
integration revealed coordinated upregulation of pancreatic islet
function coupled with suppression of lipid metabolism in both
transcriptional and chromatin accessibility profiles (Figures 3D,
S3N, and S3O), consistent with the previous report that

maturation.4 Key
\beta

NKX6.1, CHGA, and FFAR1 exhibited significant upregulation
(Figures 3E and S3P), and pathways significantly enriched by up-
regulated genes were related to
\beta

Notably, hypoxia stabilizes HIF1A to activate VEGFA-driven
angiogenesis in diverse cell types, including in tumor cells and
decidual endometrial stromal cells.88–90 However, given the

the HIF1A-mediated adaptive phase while inducing VEGFA
expression to accelerate vascularization. Importantly, HIF1A-in-

SU6656 activates VEGFA expression through an HIF1A-inde-

expression in SC-islets following either SU6656 treatment or ge-

further assessed its functional impact by co-culturing SC-islets
with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). The
HUVECs exhibited network formation, suggesting enhanced
vascularization potential. To quantitatively evaluate vascular
network growth in the co-culture system, we measured key
morphometric parameters, including the number of branches,
junctions, total length, and total segment length (Figure S4Z).
Compared with the DMSO-treated group, the SU6656-treated
SC-islets showed a significant increase in all these parameters,

SU6656 acts as a chemical inhibitor of zinc transportation to acti-

adaptation phase in SC-islet transplantation while promoting

accelerating angiogenesis during the critical early post-trans-

SU6656 protects SC-islets from apoptosis induced by
metabolic stress
Beyond overcoming short-term ischemic stress and promoting
angiogenesis, the long-term survival and functional maintenance
of SC-islets under metabolic stress are equally crucial.96 Previ-
ous studies have demonstrated that diabetic conditions upregu-
late heat shock protein 90 (HSP90) expression, and its inhibition
ameliorates metabolic stress. In order to explore whether
SU6656 can protect SC-islets from metabolic stress, we identi-
fied stress-responsive pathways functionally coupled to zinc
transportation networks based on our single-cell transcriptomic
profiling. SU6656 treatment caused coordinated downregulation
of HSP90-related and apoptotic pathways, including decreased
expression of HSP90AA1 (involved in heat shock response) and
CYCS (critical for apoptosome formation) (Figures S3T and
S5A–S5C).
Notably, HSP90 dissociates heat shock factor 1 (HSF1),
enabling its activation and subsequent initiation of pro-apoptotic
signaling cascades under stress condition.11 HSP90α and its
phosphorylation at Thr5/7 sites are directly involved in apoptosis
regulation in various cancer cell lines, demonstrating their func-
tional participation in cell apoptosis.97 Experimental validation
confirmed that SU6656 reduced basal expression of HSP90AA1
(encode HSP90) expression and suppressed zinc overload-
induced HSP90AA1 upregulation (Figures S5D and S5E).

DISCUSSION
In recent years, clinical studies have shown promising progress
in type 1 diabetes treatment with SC-islets.9,98–100 However,
functional immaturity, hypoxic vulnerability, and vascular defi-
ciency challenge SC-islet efficacy. Crucially, unlike cardiomyo-
cytes or hepatocytes that leverage HIF1A-VEGFA signaling for
hypoxia-driven angiogenesis, early
deprivation paradoxi-
cally suppresses VEGFA expression by inducing SC-islet
oxidative stress, apoptosis, and dysfunction, ultimately
compromising graft integration.12,24,25,31,32 This dual effect ad-
dresses the paradox of hypoxia-driven vascularization—while
hypoxia is necessary for VEGFA induction, early ischemic

metabolic immaturity and severely impairs hypoxia adaptation.
Our results demonstrate that inhibited zinc transportation leads
to AMPK activation, conferring the hypoxia resistance in SC-is-
lets. Chemical inhibitor of zinc transportation enables HIF1A-in-
dependent VEGFA expression even under hypoxia, thereby
accelerating graft revascularization while preserving
\beta

vival. By reconciling the dual demands of metabolic robustness
and rapid vascular integration, our findings demonstrate that
AMPK activation by chemical inhibition of zinc transportation
boosts SC-islet functional competence, offering a potential
strategy to improve the efficacy of stem cell-based therapies
for diabetes.
Improving SC-islet functionality requires hierarchical optimiza-
tion of differentiation protocols, structural mimicry, and meta-
bolic adaptation. Recent progress includes refined differentia-
tion timelines that enhance dynamic insulin secretion through
small-molecule-mediated multi-pathway modulation, coupled
with 3D architectural optimization to replicate native islet glucose
responsiveness.2,3,6,60,61,101–105 Metabolic maturation strategies
addressing mitochondrial energetics and glucose metabolism
coupling show promise in resolving insulin secretion deficits,
such as Wnt pathway modulation and ceramide homeostasis re-
modeling,4,106–108 to improve lineage specificity and SC-islet
\beta
cell maturation. Complementary strategies like circadian rhythm
synchronization further align secretory and metabolic cycles.109
Yet, these approaches do not address hypoxia tolerance and
vascularization post-transplantation for SC-islets.
Significant progress has been made in enhancing graft vascu-
larization through bioengineering-based strategies. Recent
advancements include 3D-printed vascular scaffolds with
angiogenic microchannels, hypoxia-responsive nanoparticles
for controlled VEGF delivery, and endothelial cell-laden
bioinks that promote rapid anastomosis with host vascula-
ture.
While bioengineering innovations have pioneered
structural solutions for graft vascularization, their reliance on
external scaffolds introduces inherent complexities in clinical
scalability. In parallel, emerging pharmacological strategies
that potentiate endogenous signaling pathways offer a comple-
mentary approach, coupling mechanistic simplicity with physio-
logical adaptability.120
Here, we demonstrate that chemical
inhibition of zinc transportation in SC-islets unveils a self-sus-
taining vascularization mechanism; AMPK activation not only
confers hypoxia resistance but also amplifies endogenous
VEGFA expression, which accelerates graft revascularization
and enhances glycemic control in diabetic models, without reli-
ance on external scaffolds, invasive methods, or complex
bioengineering.
In the present study, our mechanistic analyses demonstrate
that SU6656 activates AMPK in SC-islets through a zinc-depen-
dent and ZnT8-specific pathway. Genetic ablation of SLC30A8 is
sufficient to abolish zinc transportation and activate AMPK, phe-
nocopying the effect of SU6656 treatment. Importantly, in
SC-islets, where zinc transportation is largely elim-
inated, SU6656 fails to further enhance AMPK phosphorylation,
suggesting that its AMPK-activating capacity in SC-islets criti-
cally depends on inhibiting zinc transportation. These findings
establish zinc homeostasis, rather than direct kinase engage-
ment, as the dominant regulatory node through which SU6656
modulates AMPK signaling in SC-islets. Notably, SU6656 has
been reported to activate AMPK through alternative mecha-
nisms in other cell types, including Fyn kinase inhibition in murine
skeletal muscle121
and direct ATP-competitive binding to

EXPERIMENTAL MODEL AND STUDY PARTICIPANT DETAILS
Animals
All animal experiments were approved by the Biological Research Ethics Committee of Tongji University and were conducted in full
compliance with the University of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (Ethics Approval: TJAB03624102). Male
SCID-Beige mice (6–8 weeks old, weighing 20-25 g) were acquired from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd.
and were housed in the animal facility at Tongji University, Shanghai, China. All mice were maintained under specific pathogen-free
(SPF) conditions in micro-isolated cages (5 per cage) with ad libitum access to sterilized food and water. The animal room was kept at
2 2 \pm 1 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
with
relative humidity under a 12-hour light/dark cycle. The mice were experimentally naı¨ve and had received no
prior treatments. For transplantation experiments, animals were randomly allocated to different treatment groups.
Cell lines
The Mel1-Reporter cell line (INSGFP/W-NKX6.1mCherry/mCherry) was generously provided by Prof. Xin Chen from the Shanghai Institute
of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, China.
and ZnT8::RFP reporter hESC lines were constructed following the
methodology described in our laboratory’s previously published study.43,57 The HuES8 cell line was a generous donation from
Prof. Qiurong Ding at the Shanghai Institute of Nutrition and Health. The new non-reporter human embryonic stem AD-B cell line
was established by the Stem Cell Bank of Shanghai East Hospital.
HuES8 and AD-B cell line were constructed according
to the method described below. P3 Swiss Webster mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) were derived in-house through standard
protocols, with the Swiss Webster mice used for their derivation kindly gifted by Prof. Xin Chen.

pital of Naval Medical University. The study was approved by the institutional ethics committee (Ethics Approval: CHEC2018-111),
and all donors provided written informed consent. Isolation procedures followed modified protocols adapted from the Clinical Islet
Transplantation consortium guidelines.123,124 Briefly, peripancreatic adipose and connective tissues were dissected from fresh pan-
creata, followed by multi-point ice-cold perfusion using

AD-B and HuES8 hESC lines were engineered using CRISPR-Cas9. A single-guide RNA targeting exon 4 was designed

Library construction and NGS sequencing
scRNA-seq
For WT SC-islets, the scRNA-seq libraries were generated with the DNBelab C Series Single-Cell Library Prep Kit V3.0 (MGI, #940-
001818-00). For WFS1-/- SC-islets, the scRNA-seq libraries were generated with the DNBelab C Series Single-Cell Library Prep Kit
V2.0 (MGI, #940-000519-00). Key steps involved droplet-based cell capture, emulsion disruption, cDNA synthesis through reverse
transcription, and PCR amplification of cDNA and oligonucleotide products. Library preparation was performed with a target cellular
input of 30,000 cells per experimental group, adhering to the standardized protocols provided by the manufacturer. For quality con-
trol, library concentrations were measured using the Qubit ssDNA Detection Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, #Q10212). Final
sequencing was conducted on the DNBSEQ-T7 platform (MGI Technologies) at the JMDNA sequencing facility.
snATAC-seq
snATAC-seq were performed by Jiayin Biotechnology Ltd. The 10x Genomics Single Cell ATAC v1.1 Kit was used for snATAC-seq
library preparation in accordance with the manufacturer’s guidelines. Briefly, nuclei were isolated from SC-islets, and cell viability was

the 10x Genomics Chromium Single Cell ATAC Reagent Kits User Guide.
scRNA-seq data analysis
scRNA-seq on WT SC-islets
Raw sequencing data were aligned to the GRCh38 human reference genome. Low-quality cells were filtered out by applying the
following thresholds: cells with fewer than 200 or more than 9,000 detected genes, and cells with mitochondrial gene content
exceeding
. Genes expressed in fewer than 3 cells per sample were excluded.
We normalized the data using the SCTransform v2 method. SelectIntegrationFeatures was used to set variable features. Principal
component analysis was performed using the RunPCA function, with the top 40 PCs retained for cell clustering and downstream
analysis. FindNeighbors and FindClusters (the resolution was adjusted to 0.1) functions were run. We performed dimensionality
reduction using the RunUMAP function.
To define cell types, marker genes for each cluster were identified with the FindAllMarkers function. Based on the expression of
known marker genes (GCG, INS, SST, FEV, SOX9, TOP2A), the distinct clusters were identified and annotated as
\alpha
-cells,
\beta
-cells,
δ-cells, enterochromaffin cells, progenitor cells, and proliferating cells.
We compared the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) levels in
\beta
-cells between DMSO and SU6656. DEGs were identified using
FindMarkers function in Seurat with adjusted
p
-value
and |log2(fold change)
as cutoffs. Pathway enrichment analysis
was performed using the GO and Reactome databases via the R package clusterProfiler.
scRNA-seq on WFS1-/- SC-islets
We performed the similar strategy that described in the previous section.
snATAC-seq data analysis
Sequencing reads were processed through the Cell Ranger ATAC pipeline and aligned to the GRCh38-1.1.0 reference genome for
mapping, de-duplication, and identification of Tn5 cut sites. The objects were generated from Signac. We kept cells of which mapped
ATAC-seq fragments more than 500 and less than 1200, TSS enrichment score more than 4 and the approximate ratio of mononu-
cleosomal to nucleosome-free fragments less than 1.2. The doublet score was calculated by scDblFinder package using scDblFinder
function. We performed latent semantic indexing (LSI) to reduce dimension. We clustered cells and added UMAP embedding through
FindNeighbors and FindClusters.
snATAC-seq and scRNA-seq dataset were integrated. We initially estimated gene activity scores from snATAC-seq data using the
GeneActivity function, followed by log-normalization. To integrate the datasets, we compared the gene activity scores derived from
snATAC-seq with gene expression profiles from scRNA-seq. This was achieved using the IntegrateLayers function, which employed
Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) for dimension reduction and alignment. We identified cell types based on gene markers
mentioned below. Differential accessible genes (DAGs) were calculated using FindMarkers function based on gene activity scores.
Transcription factor activity analysis
Transcription factor activity was performed using chromVAR. The position weight matrix was obtained from JASPAR2020 database.
Per-cell motif activity score was computed by RunChromVAR function in Signac. We calculated differential activity by using
FindMarkers function.
